sourceMovie.title: Requiem for a Dream
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit Requiem for a Dream for the same reason it broke through in 2000: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a drama rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Darren Aronofsky keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The crime and drama blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Trainspotting
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Requiem for a Dream clicked for you, Trainspotting (1996) is a strong next stop: both trade in crime and drama, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Requiem for a Dream?
faq.answer: Try Trainspotting, American Gangster, Blow—each shares DNA with Requiem for a Dream while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Requiem for a Dream?
faq.answer: Requiem for a Dream (2000) is best described as Crime, Drama. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Requiem for a Dream worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 8.0/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this crime lane.
